Caption: Begint je AI-agent vaker fouten te maken? Dan denk je misschien dat prompt engineering de oplossing is. Maar het probleem zit eigenlijk ergens anders: Er is een vaardigheid die veel meer impact heeft en waar nog weinig over gesproken wordt, namelijk context engineering. De twee klinken gelijkaardig, maar ze spelen zich op een heel ander niveau af. ➡ Prompt engineering gaat alleen over hoe je je prompt opstelt. ➡ Context engineering gaat over alles wat een AI-agent weet gedurende een volledige werksessie, en hoe je dat actief beheert. Moderne AI-modellen hebben een context window van zo'n 200.000 tokens. Dat klinkt ruim, tot je ziet hoe snel die volloopt. Een agent die tegelijk je codebase analyseert, tools aanroept, gesprekshistoriek bijhoudt en instructies onthoudt, vult die ruimte sneller dan je denkt. Veel developers zullen het gevoel herkennen. Je werkt al een tijdje met je agent, en code die hij een halfuur geleden nog correct schreef, zit nu vol fouten. Hij lijkt de instructies te vergeten die je aan het begin van de sessie meegaf. Dus pas je je prompt aan, maar het wordt er niet beter op. Het voelt alsof de tool gewoon onbetrouwbaar is, maar eigenlijk zit de context window gewoon te vol om nog goed te kunnen redeneren. Er is een drempel, ruwweg 40% van de context window, waarna de kwaliteit merkbaar daalt. Ontwikkelaars noemen dat de "Dumb Zone". De meeste mensen weten niet dat die drempel bestaat, en werken er daardoor de hele tijd tegenaan zonder te begrijpen wat er misgaat. De oplossing zit in begrijpen wat er in die context window zit, wat eruit moet, en hoe je een agent structureert zodat hij gefocust blijft. Een andere prompt schrijven lost het probleem niet op. We schreven er een uitgebreide blog over met concrete technieken om je context window goed te beheren. 👉 Lees het op onze blog. Link in bio. ContextEngineering AIAgents AIDrivenDevelopment

Caption: Your AI has six doors into your business. Most companies have only ever locked the front one. When most people think about AI security, they think about keeping attackers out. Firewalls. Authentication. Access controls. The front door. But AI systems don't operate through a single door. They interact with users, consume data, connect to tools, store context, integrate with third-party systems, and influence business processes. Each of those becomes a potential entry point. Here are six doors every organization should be paying attention to: 1. The Prompt Door Anyone who can interact with your AI can attempt to influence its behavior. 2. The Data Door AI systems learn from and rely on the information they receive. Poor or manipulated data can create poor outcomes. 3. The Tool Door Every API, database, email platform, or business system connected to AI expands its ability to take action. 4. The Memory Door What your AI remembers can be just as important as what it knows. Retained context creates both value and risk. 5. The Integration Door Third-party applications, plugins, and connectors often introduce risks organizations never evaluate. 6. The Human Door Employees remain one of the largest attack surfaces. Shadow AI usage, unmanaged tools, and poor practices can bypass even the strongest technical controls. The mistake many organizations make is securing the model while ignoring the ecosystem around it. But attackers rarely target the strongest point They look for the easiest door. The question isn't: "Is our AI secure?" The better question is: "Which of these six doors is currently open?" Because AI security isn't about protecting a model. It's about securing every pathway that can influence its decisions and actions. 📌 Save this post and use it as a checklist during your next AI security review. Caption: One of the biggest automation mistakes I see is jumping to the solution too early. How much does a palletiser cost? How much does a capping machine cost? What's the budget? They're all reasonable questions. The problem is that they're often asked before we've fully understood what needs to be achieved. What product? What throughput? What level of flexibility? What labour challenges are we trying to solve? What does success actually look like? It's tempting to jump straight to solutions and budgets. But that's where projects can start on the wrong foot. The wrong assumptions get made. The wrong expectations get set. And sometimes the wrong solution gets pursued altogether. The best automation projects I've been involved in all started the same way. Not with a machine. Not with a budget. With a problem. Understand the problem properly and the right solution usually becomes much easier to find. automation manufacturing engineering industrialautomation problemsolving processevolution

Caption: These are 9 DevOps mistakes that show up repeatedly in startups. Some are expensive. Some are embarrassing. A few can take down entire systems. 1. One AWS account for everything Dev, staging, and production in the same account creates unnecessary risk. A dev misconfiguration can directly impact production or cost. Separate accounts early. 2. No billing alerts Without cost monitoring, cloud spend becomes invisible until it’s too late. A single Auto Scaling misconfiguration once triggered 50 instances instead of 5 for days. Result: a $12,000 bill. 3. Over-engineering before launch Kubernetes, service meshes, and complex infrastructure before product-market fit. Most early-stage systems fail from complexity, not lack of scale. 4. Hardcoded AWS credentials Embedding credentials in code or repositories is a critical security risk. Secrets should be managed properly from day one (not “later”). 5. Public SSH access (0.0.0.0/0 on port 22) Exposing SSH to the internet guarantees exposure to automated brute-force attacks. Session Manager or restricted access should be the default. 6. Deploying on Friday afternoons Production changes close to weekends increase risk and slow response time if something breaks. Deployment timing is part of reliability strategy. 7. No rollback strategy Every deployment should answer one question: How is this reverted in minutes if it breaks production? Without that, deployments are high-risk events. 8. No monitoring before launch Monitoring after an outage is reactive, not operational maturity. CloudWatch alarms and health checks should exist before production traffic. 9. Single point of failure everywhere Single EC2, single AZ, single NAT Gateway introduces avoidable downtime risk. Systems should assume components will fail not behave perfectly. Most production incidents are not caused by complex failures. They come from basic infrastructure decisions made too early or too casually. Strong DevOps practice is often just avoiding these fundamentals. devops aws cloudcomputing sre systemdesign

Caption: Patch day. Two words that can trigger anything from mild annoyance to full-blown panic depending on how old your infrastructure is. Most teams don't intentionally neglect updates. They're busy shipping features, onboarding customers and fixing production issues. Updating third-party infrastructure components often ends up somewhere at the bottom of the backlog, right next to all those things everyone agrees are important but nobody has time for. The problem is that technical debt compounds. Maybe your Kubernetes version is a few releases behind. Your Helm charts haven't been updated in months. That Terraform provider still works alright, so why check it? Then eventually the day comes when you actually need to upgrade something and suddenly you're not upgrading one version. You're upgrading ten or more. I've seen this happen more than once. What should have been a routine maintenance task turned into a project spanning several weeks because too many dependencies had drifted too far apart. The longer you delay, the more painful it becomes. That's why I'm a big fan of continuous upgrades. Not because upgrading software is exciting. It isn't. But reviewing ten small changes every week is significantly easier than reviewing three hundred changes once per year. Tools like Renovate help automate a large part of that process. Instead of manually checking every dependency, container image, Terraform provider or Helm chart, Renovate continuously scans for updates and creates pull requests automatically. You still stay in control. Nothing gets deployed without review, but your team no longer has to remember what needs updating and when. The result is usually not only just better security, but also less operational risk. Because patch days stop being special events. They become part of normal operations. How are you currently handling updates for third-party infrastructure components? Are you already using Renovate or are upgrades still mostly manual? Caption: ⚡ Most data engineers use Spark every day — but freeze when asked: "What does the Driver actually do?" Here's the truth nobody told you clearly: Spark has a "Brain" and a "Muscle." 🧠 DRIVER = The Brain → Converts your code into a DAG (execution plan) → Schedules tasks across the cluster → Tracks which executor is alive and which crashed → Never processes a single row of data itself 💪 EXECUTOR = The Muscle → Actually runs your transformations & actions → Caches data in memory for speed → Sends heartbeats to the Driver every few seconds → Lives on worker nodes across the cluster Here's what nobody talks about: If your Driver dies → your entire job dies. It holds the SparkContext. If one Executor dies → Spark just reruns that task elsewhere. No drama. Knowing this one thing changes how you debug, tune, and architect every Spark application you'll ever write. I wrote a deep-dive article that explains the whole Driver–Executor duo in a way that will actually stick. 📖 Link in bio → Medium: Spark Driver vs Executor 🔔 Follow for weekly content on Data Engineering, Spark, and Big Data that cuts through the noise. ▶️ Subscribe to my YouTube for visual walkthroughs → ApacheSpark DataEngineering SparkTutorial PySpark BigDataEngineer LearnDataEngineering TechTips DataEngineerLife SparkArchitecture Databricks 100DaysOfCode

Caption: 📅 Dia 08 de 30 do Desafio de dados Databricks Professional ✨ Auto Loader Master: Schema Evolution e Detecção de Drift ​Abrindo a segunda semana do desafio com o pé no acelerador em Ingestão Escalável e Streaming! Hoje o foco total foi dominar o Auto Loader e aprender a lidar com uma das maiores dores de cabeça na engenharia de dados: a mudança repentina na estrutura dos arquivos que chegam no Data Lake. ​📌 O Poder do Auto Loader (cloudFiles) ​Ingestão Incremental Eficiente: Ele descobre novos arquivos assim que eles chegam no S3/ADLS de forma absurdamente eficiente, usando notificação de arquivos ou listagem otimizada, sem precisar reprocessar o que já passou. ​Schema Evolution (Evolução de Esquema): Em vez de o pipeline quebrar no meio da madrugada porque uma nova coluna surgiu, o Auto Loader consegue detectar a mudança e evoluir o schema automaticamente (cloudFiles.schemaEvolutionMode), adaptando a tabela Delta sem intervenção manual. ​Detecção de Schema Drift com _rescued_data: Se uma coluna nova aparecer ou um tipo de dado mudar drasticamente, o Auto Loader joga essa informação para uma coluna especial de "dados resgatados". Isso garante que o pipeline continue rodando e nenhum dado seja perdido enquanto você analisa a mudança. ​✨ Reflexão Diária No mundo real, os dados são vivos e mudam o tempo todo. Um pipeline verdadeiramente resiliente não é aquele que assume que o dado sempre virá perfeito, mas sim aquele que sabe se adaptar ao "drift" de forma inteligente e segura. Caption: Why the data system your company built for basic reporting is quietly failing your AI. 🛑🖥️ Most corporations organize their modern data lakes using a pattern called the "Medallion Architecture," which filters raw info through three separate steps (Bronze, Silver, and Gold). While this is great for basic charts and graphs, it creates a massive blind spot for advanced AI features like search bots and automated assistants. When an AI model hallucinated or retrieves the wrong document piece, your developers need to know exactly where and when that individual row of data was corrupted. Traditional systems can only track large groups of tables, making finding a single broken record look like searching for a needle in a haystack. Upgrading to Apache Iceberg v3 introduces native Row Lineage. This smart update tags every single row with its own digital passport (_row_id), allowing your engineers to instantly query the life history of any data record with 100% precision. Stop trying to power tomorrow's AI on yesterday's blueprints. Learn how to architect elite, crystal-clear enterprise data platforms at DataEngineering ApacheIceberg DataOps

Caption: CASE STUDY: ONLINE COACH 📈 An online coach had a problem many businesses secretly struggle with. People were visiting her website every day. Traffic wasn't the issue. The real question was: **"If people are visiting my website, why aren't they buying?"** Hundreds of visitors would come. They would read about her programs. Browse her services. Then leave. No enquiry. No purchase. No follow-up. Then she realized something important: **Most people don't buy the first time they see your offer.** People need time. People need trust. People need reminders. So instead of trying to sell immediately, she changed her strategy. She created a simple **5-day email nurture sequence.** For five days, she stayed connected with her audience. 📩 Day 1: Welcome email and her story 📩 Day 2: Valuable tips and practical advice 📩 Day 3: Client success stories and testimonials 📩 Day 4: Solutions to common objections 📩 Day 5: A clear offer with a call to action She stopped chasing customers. She started building relationships. The results after just 30 days were impressive: 📧 48% email open rate 📈 27% increase in sales The biggest lesson? 💡 **Follow-up creates conversions.** Many businesses lose customers not because their product is bad, but because they stop the conversation too early. Remember this: ❌ Website visitors are not lost opportunities. ❌ People rarely buy the first time they see you. ✅ Consistent follow-up builds trust. ✅ Trust creates sales. The fortune is often in the follow-up. 👇 Be honest: Are you following up with your potential customers? Comment **YES** or **NO** below. 💾 Save this post and share it with a business owner who needs this reminder. Caption: The game changed. The foundation didn't. Search used to be about ranking on Google. Now it's about being trusted by AI. But here's what most marketers are getting wrong: they're treating GEO (Generative Engine Optimisation) and SEO as a choice — pick one, skip the other. That's not how it works. → GEO without SEO means AI has no reason to trust you. No domain authority, no structured content, no backlink credibility — AI models skip right past you when forming answers. → SEO without GEO means you rank on Google, but you're invisible when someone asks ChatGPT, Perplexity, or Gemini a question your business should be answering. You don't get to choose one layer. Both need to run simultaneously, or you're already losing ground to competitors who figured this out. The brands winning in AI-era search built their SEO foundation first — then layered GEO on top: structured answers, cited content, E-E-A-T signals, semantic clarity. The question isn't SEO or GEO. It's: are you running both — and are they talking to each other? SEO GEO AIMarketing SearchStrategy GenerativeSearch

Caption: MJF a Riccardo Donadon: un riconoscimento a chi trasforma l'innovazione in valore per la comunità. Roberto Limitone, Governatore del Distretto 108Ta3, ha conferito la Melvin Jones Fellow a Riccardo Donadon, Amministratore Delegato e fondatore di H-FARM. Un riconoscimento che premia non solo una brillante carriera imprenditoriale, capace di fare di H-FARM uno dei principali ecosistemi europei dedicati all'innovazione, all'economia digitale e alla formazione, ma anche una visione che pone la tecnologia al servizio delle persone. Attraverso il sostegno alla nascita di start-up innovative e di progetti ad alto impatto sociale, Riccardo Donadon ha saputo promuovere modelli di sviluppo fondati sulla solidarietà, sull'inclusione e sulla creazione di nuove opportunità per le giovani generazioni e per le comunità. Congratulazioni a Riccardo Donadon per questo prestigioso riconoscimento. Caption: Hey fam! Last night, we joined AI Tinkerers Hong Kong & GBA at Dialogue in the Dark, a great evening about building AI that genuinely enables. 🤖 The night opened the way it should: with the people AI is meant to serve. Visually impaired hosts from Dialogue in the Dark shared how AI is already reshaping their daily lives and, just as importantly, the real-world UX problems they still need engineers to solve. A grounding reminder that accessibility isn’t a feature you bolt on at the end; it’s the brief. 🙂 From there, the demos. Three teams shared technical work spanning two accessibility projects and a live translation app - the kind of “does it actually work in the wild?” building we love to see. AWS & Kiro also dropped in for a session (and kept the room fed 🙏). What struck us most was the crowd: deeply technical, hands-on, the kind of people training their own models and hacking on hard problems for the right reasons. Exactly the energy we really love being around! 😉 At OAX Foundation, this is the intersection we care about: AI that extends human capability and reaches the people who need it most. It’s also why we back projects like mapping the people, ideas, and events moving Asia’s AI scene forward. Nights like this are a big part of that story. 🥰 Huge thanks to Dialogue in the Dark for hosting, and to AI Tinkerers HK & GBA for convening such a thoughtful room. More of this, please! 🥂 tech startup hongkong
Author: oax_foundation

Caption: You know when you type a question into your LLM (let's say Claude), and it says...thinking... Or, it gives you the impression it's thinking. Well, it's not. Your AI is a predication engine. It doesn't have thoughts, or feelings for that matter. It has lots and lots of data and it uses that to predict the most likely answer. This means, it doesn't always get it right, which is why fact checking is so important. Being honest, have you started to rely on AI a bit like it's a friend? Seriously no judgement! 😊 It's easy to do, that's what tech companies are trying to achieve in their interfaces and responses. Try this instead. Use your LLM to help challenge you. To broaden your cognitive skills, not lessen them. Try sparring with your AI model on a subject you know about and whilst doing so, remember, it's a prediction engine, it's not really thinking and you can't hurt its feelings. AISolutions AIConsultant AIStrategy AutomationExpert DigitalTransformation

Caption: AI can do a lot, but it can't carry responsibility for your decisions. That's why the smartest teams are building AI and human judgement side by side, on purpose. AI is great at the heavy lifting: analysing data, spotting patterns, and drafting options in seconds. Humans are great at the trust work: nuance, empathy, ethical reasoning, and accountability. So before you roll out another tool, get clear on roles. What will AI automate, what will it advise on, and what must a person approve every time? Next, build a workflow where AI outputs come with a review moment, not an auto-send button. If the decision affects people, money, safety, or privacy, add stronger oversight and clear escalation paths. Keep the loop tight so your AI improves responsibly: create, deploy, measure, review, refine, redeploy. And reinvest the time you save into the skills that compound, like critical thinking, communication, and great leadership. Our principle at Maybe* is simple: let AI banish repetitive tasks so your humans can shine at strategy and creativity. If you want a practical starting point, book a call with a Maybe* expert and we'll help you choose a safe pilot and scale with confidence. Start with one task. Head to our bio to learn more. Caption: E SE UN'AZIENDA NON AVESSE PIÙ UN AMMINISTRATORE, MA UN'INTELLIGENZA ARTIFICIALE? In Argentina si discute una proposta di legge rivoluzionaria: riconoscere società gestite esclusivamente da sistemi di Intelligenza Artificiale ( È la domanda che si pone Yuval Noah Harari (filosofo israeliano, uno dei più grandi guru dell’IA) non è poi così banale (🔗 Sito ufficiale di Yuval Noah Harari). Oggi le aziende sono già "persone giuridiche": possono possedere beni, firmare contr tti, fare causa. Ma dietro ogni decisione c'è, almeno in teoria, una persona che ne risponde. Domani potrebbe non essere più così. Un'Intelligenza Artificiale può prendere decisioni, ottimizzare processi, gestire investimenti, negoziare contratti. Ma se sbaglia, chi ne risponde? Un algoritmo non prova senso di colpa, non teme una condanna, non ha coscienza morale. La questione, quindi, non è solo tecnologica. È giuridica, economica ed etica. Questo però non significa chiudere la porta per principio. Non dobbiamo affrontare l’Intelligenza Artificiale con paura o pregiudizio culturale. Se ben regolata, trasparente e orientata al bene comune, una nuova forma di organizzazione assistita dall’IA potrebbe anche produrre efficienza, ridurre errori, migliorare servizi e generare vantaggi reali per la società umana. Harari, come spesso accade, spinge il ragionamento fino alle conseguenze più estreme, immaginando un futuro in cui grandi organizzazioni governate dall'IA possano acquisire un potere enorme. Che questo scenario si realizzi o meno, una cosa è certa: mentre impariamo a costruire macchine sempre più intelligenti, dobbiamo imparare anche a costruire regole altrettanto intelligenti. Perché il vero problema non è che l'Intelligenza Artificiale prenda decisioni. È capire chi sarà responsabile delle loro conseguenze. 🔗 Financial Times – *We must not grant AI agents legal personhood* IntelligenzaArtificiale AI YuvalNoahHarari Harari Innovazione EticaDigitale AIGovernance Responsabilità Diritto Tecnologia FutureOfWork AgenticAI AIAgents CorporateGovernance

Caption: Most "AI marketing" tools are automation wearing an AI label. One line separates the real thing from the costume. We just moved TheNextGuide's Instagram engine from automated to AI-native — a tourism marketplace with more than 2,400 operators and 18,000 tours. The automated version ran the same play every day and never learned from it. The AI-native version closes a feedback loop: posts produce metrics, metrics rewrite the next cycle, and the team approves the system's changes to itself. The line between the two comes down to one thing — whether the loop closes, and who approves what it changes. A fancier model with no loop is still automation with a better filter. So we wrote a four-question audit you can run on any tool being pitched to you. 💬 Which side of the line is your current stack on? Save the audit and run it on the next pitch. Caption: Stop sending emails that start with "Hi founder at Acme Corp, Inc." Standard CRM enrichment APIs are lazy. They grab raw legal names, outdated LinkedIn titles, and messy descriptions. If you map those directly to your outreach sequence, your conversion rate dies a silent death. You don't need a massive engineering team to fix this. You just need a 3-step self-correcting no-code pipeline. Here is the exact stack we deployed to clean 10k+ leads automatically: 1. Clay or Apollo for raw data retrieval. 2. A Make.com scenario routing to Claude 3.5 Sonnet to clean company names (e.g., stripping "LLC", "Group", "Inc.") and translating complex titles into plain English. 3. A smart router that flags low-confidence enrichments and sends them to a "Human-in-the-Loop" Airtable queue instead of the automated sequencing tool. Caption: The person who built Claude Code runs 10 to 15 sessions at once. 🚀 Boris Cherny (Anthropic, creator of Claude Code) shared his exact workflow. His CLAUDE.md is around 100 lines — outperforming 800-line configs. Here's the 6-step loop he actually uses: Step 1: Plan first, code second Step 2: Run sessions in parallel git worktrees Step 3: Give Claude a way to verify itself Step 4: Run /code-review before every merge Step 5: Challenge it, don't babysit Step 6: Turn every mistake into a rule in CLAUDE.md Slide 7 is the one most people skip. Caption: Multinational technology and e-commerce giant Amazon has announced plans to invest a massive $48 billion in India between 2026 and 2030, following a strategic meeting between CEO Andy Jassy and Prime Minister Narendra Modi. The investment includes an additional $13 billion dedicated to significantly expanding the company's artificial intelligence (AI) and cloud computing infrastructure across the country by the end of the decade. This latest commitment comes about six months after Amazon announced plans to invest $35 billion in the country, highlighting the tech major's long-term focus on India as a critical market for digital infrastructure, cloud computing, and AI development. Alongside its deep investments in cloud and AI infrastructure, Amazon is aggressively accelerating its quick commerce push in the country through Amazon Now. The company plans to scale the rapid delivery service to over 100 cities and build a robust network of 1,000 micro-fulfillment centers. This operational expansion comes as competition in India's quick commerce sector intensifies, with major players rapidly widening their offerings beyond groceries to categories such as electronics, beauty, fashion, and household essentials through near-instant delivery. Caption: The Startup DevOps Checklist. Not for reading once. For reviewing monthly. Because most outages don’t come from unknown problems, they come from skipped basics. Foundation ↳ Root account MFA enabled ↳ CloudTrail enabled across all regions ↳ Billing alerts set (50%, 80%, 100%) ↳ Separate AWS accounts for dev / staging / prod ↳ Infrastructure managed as code (Terraform only) ↳ Remote state stored in S3 with DynamoDB locking / S3 native lock ↳ Pre-commit hooks enabled (no exposed secrets in git) Security ↳ No production IAM users with AdministratorAccess ↳ All secrets stored in Secrets Manager / Parameter Store ↳ No public exposure of ports (except 80/443) ↳ RDS deployed in private subnets only ↳ GuardDuty enabled across all regions ↳ Container/image scanning in CI/CD (e.g., Trivy) Pipeline ↳ CI/CD triggered on every commit to main ↳ Stages include: lint → test → build → scan → deploy ↳ Images tagged with commit SHA (never “latest”) ↳ Rollback procedure documented and tested ↳ No manual production deployments ↳ Deployment freeze after Friday 12pm Operations ↳ CloudWatch alarms for CPU, memory, errors, latency ↳ Auto Scaling Group health checks configured properly ↳ Application health checks in boot/user data flows ↳ Log retention policies defined ↳ Runbooks exist for top failure scenarios ↳ Postmortem process defined and ready Most production incidents are not caused by missing advanced tools. They are caused by missing basics that were never enforced. How many of these are currently in place? The gaps are usually not theoretical. They are where the next incident will come from. devops aws cloudcomputing sre systemdesign

Caption: "아무리 좋은 AI라도, 우리 회사 현장에 안 맞으면 무용지물입니다." ​ 이론적인 기술 소개보다 인포유앤컴퍼니를 가장 잘 이해하는 방법은 실제 고객사의 현장 이야기입니다. 업종도, 규모도 다르지만 인포유와 함께 명확한 AX 성과를 이뤄낸 3가지 실전 사례를 요약해 드립니다. ​ ⚡ 사례 1. 에너지·중공업 대기업 D사 Problem: 임직원의 외부 AI 무단 사용으로 인한 기밀 유출 리스크, 반복적인 수기 정산 지연. Solution: 그룹 인프라 내 Private AI 보안 환경 구축 및 문서 자동화 시스템 연결. Result: 외부 AI 차단 및 보안 완전 전환, 수작업 프로세스 제로화. ​ 🌍 사례 2. 가전·반도체 글로벌 탑티어 S사 Problem: 전 세계 23개 채널의 VOC 데이터 사일로, 현업의 낮은 데이터 접근성. Solution: 단일 저장소 기반 준실시간 대용량 처리 및 셀프 서비스 분석 환경 설계. Result: 의사결정 속도 향상, IT 의존 없는 데이터 민주화 실현. ​ 🌱 사례 3. 국제 아동권리 NGO S사 Problem: 데이터 정합성 불일치, 빈번한 시스템 장애, 5개년 대용량 통합 조회 불가능. Solution: 데이터 정제·이력 추적 체계 재설계 및 실시간 모니터링 알람 구축. Result: 보고서 수치 정확성 보장, 5개년 대용량 데이터 통합 인프라 수립. ​ ​ 100건 이상의 프로젝트를 수행하며 인포유앤컴퍼니가 깨달은 단 하나는, 데이터 문제와 AI 문제를 분리하지 않고 '하나의 흐름'으로 연결해야 진짜 비즈니스 임팩트가 생긴다는 점입니다. ​ 각 기업의 복잡한 난제를 인포유가 구체적으로 어떤 기술 아키텍처(Azure, Databricks 등)로 뚫어냈는지, 상세한 실전 케이스는 공식 블로그에서 확인하실 수 있습니다. The fear that departmental hardware will “burden down” or expose corporate ERP is real. And that's exactly where PORTANT 5.0 comes in. PORTANT 5.0 is the evolution of our proprietary middleware, designed to act as a true "shock absorber" and filter between the hardware in the field and the company's IT infrastructure. It does not just transmit data, but governs the entire architecture with three fundamental tasks: 🛡️ MAXIMUM COMPUTER SECURITY: Isolate the industrial network from the corporate ERP. Portant 5.0 communicates with antennas and field devices securely, preventing potential hardware anomalies from impacting central systems. 🌪️ ELECTRONIC "NOISE" FILTER: An RFID gate can read thousands of tags per second, generating a huge and mirrored amount of raw data. Portant 5.0 cleans and normalizes this flow in real time: it eliminates double or spurious readings and sends only the clean, perfect and ready-to-use data to the management system. 🔌 ARCHITECTURE STABILITY: If the management system suffers a slowdown or scheduled maintenance, Portant 5.0 does not lose a single piece of data. Caption: Your dashboard's 7-day average has been a running total this whole time. Write avg(sales) OVER (ORDER BY day) with no frame and Postgres defaults to RANGE UNBOUNDED PRECEDING — it averages every row from the start, not a sliding window. Your "moving average" slowly flattens into a cumulative total and quietly understates every recent trend. The fix is one clause: ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW pins a fixed 7-row window for a true moving average. Same trap applies to SUM, MIN and MAX — always state the frame. Save this for the next time a chart looks "smoothed" for no reason. Follow for daily SQL & dev one-liners. sql postgres postgresql windowfunctions database dataengineering sqltips coding

Caption: The hidden cost of "it works on my local machine" usually shows up as an expensive cloud bill in production. 💸 When you are joining tables in Apache Spark, how often do you actually check how Spark is handling it? If your answer is "never," your data pipelines are likely suffering from severe network bottlenecks. Let's demystify Spark's two primary join strategies: 1️⃣ Broadcast Hash Join (BHJ): The ultimate cheat code. If one of your dataframes is small (under 10MB by default), Spark duplicates it across all worker nodes. Zero shuffling over the network. It's blindingly fast, but try it on a large dataset and you’ll instantly trigger an OOM error. 💥 2️⃣ Sort Merge Join (SMJ): The reliable workhorse. When dealing with two massive datasets, Spark shuffles, sorts, and merges them. It's slower because moving data across network nodes takes time, but it handles massive scale without breaking a sweat. 🏋️‍♂️ The magic happens when you know how to configure spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold or use explicit broadcast hints in your code to turn a sluggish pipeline into a high-performance engine. Ready to level up your Data Engineering game? I’ve mapped out the exact architecture, internal mechanics, and code snippets for both strategies. 🔗 Links to the full video tutorial and technical article are in my bio! Check them out to optimize your next pipeline. 👉 Follow for daily engineering insights, architecture patterns, and big data tips. Caption: SQL JOINS are one of the most important concepts for anyone who wants to become a Data Analyst, SQL Developer, or Database Professional. In real-world projects, data is stored across multiple tables. SQL JOINS help you combine that data to generate meaningful reports and business insights. In this learning post, you'll understand: ✅ INNER JOIN – Returns matching records from both tables ✅ LEFT JOIN – Returns all records from the left table and matching records from the right table ✅ RIGHT JOIN – Returns all records from the right table and matching records from the left table ✅ FULL JOIN – Returns all matching and non-matching records from both tables ✅ CROSS JOIN – Returns all possible combinations of records Mastering SQL JOINS is essential for database querying, reporting, business intelligence, and interview preparation. 🎯 Learn SQL with Real-Time Projects 👨‍🏫 Expert Trainers 💻 Hands-on Training 📜 Certificate of Completion 💼 Placement Support 📞 Call / WhatsApp: **89390 69135** 📍 West Mambalam, Chennai 🌐 [ SQL SQLJoins LearnSQL Database MySQL SQLServer DataAnalysis BusinessAnalytics DataAnalyst DatabaseDeveloper BusinessIntelligence Reporting SQLTraining Programming SoftwareDeveloper TechSkills CareerGrowth Students Freshers Chennai WestMambalam SuccessRootTechnologies ITTraining JobReadySkills DataEngineering

Caption: AI içerik bolluğu başladı. Ama asıl problem daha fazla içerik üretilmesi değil. Asıl problem şu: Markalar yavaş yavaş birbirine benzemeye başladı. Son dönemde ajans-marka toplantılarında aynı cümleleri daha sık duyuyorum: “Daha hızlı üretelim.” “Daha çok alternatif çıkaralım.” “AI ile içerik akışını hızlandıralım.” “Biraz daha premium olsun.” “Biraz daha sosyal medya gibi dursun.” İlk bakışta hepsi mantıklı. Ama burada çok sinsi bir risk var. Aynı prompt mantığı, aynı brief dili, aynı “samimi ama profesyonel” ton, aynı görsel kalıplar… Bir süre sonra markaları farklılaştırmıyor. Tam tersine, aynılaştırıyor. Bence AI kötü içerik üretmiyor. Daha tehlikelisini yapıyor: Ortalama iyi içerik üretiyor. Düzgün görünüyor. Hatasız duruyor. Sunumda fena görünmüyor. Ama kimseye ait hissettirmiyor. Ajans tarafı hızı ilerleme sanıyor. Marka tarafı çok alternatifi güven sanıyor. Ama kimse yeterince şu soruyu sormuyor: “Bu fikir gerçekten bizim markamıza mı ait, yoksa sadece iyi yazılmış mı?” 2026’da en çok içerik üreten marka değil, kendi sesini en iyi koruyan marka kazanacak. Çünkü içerik bolluğunda herkes görünür olabilir. Ama herkes hatırlanmaz. Kaydet. Bir sonraki brief toplantısında bu soruyu masaya koy. Sence AI içerik bolluğu en çok neyi bozuyor? Marka tonunu mu, özgünlüğü mü, yoksa stratejik bakışı mı? fikirperest WellnessMarketing InfluencerMarketing İçerikStratejisi dijitalpazarlama

Caption: 𝗦𝘁𝗲𝗽𝘀 𝘁𝗼 𝗗𝗲𝗹𝗶𝘃𝗲𝗿 𝗮𝗻 𝗠𝗩𝗣 🚀 Many startups fail because they try to build a perfect product before validating the idea. An MVP (Minimum Viable Product) is not about building everything. It’s about building the smallest version of a product that solves a real problem and gets real user feedback. Here’s the process I follow when delivering an MVP: ✅ 1. Understand the Problem Before writing a single line of code, understand the user’s pain points, business goals, and target audience. ✅ 2. Define Core Features List all features and identify the 20% that deliver 80% of the value. Everything else can wait. ✅ 3. Create Wireframes & User Flow Map out how users will interact with the product. A clear user journey saves countless development hours. ✅ 4. Design the UI/UX Create a simple, clean, and intuitive interface. MVPs don’t need fancy animations—they need usability. ✅ 5. Build the Database Structure A well-planned database prevents major issues when scaling later. ✅ 6. Develop Core Functionality Focus only on the features required for launch. Avoid feature creep. ✅ 7. Testing & Debugging Test every workflow, edge case, and user action. Reliability matters more than having extra features. ✅ 8. Launch Quickly Get the product into the hands of real users as soon as possible. ✅ 9. Collect Feedback Listen to users, analyze behavior, and identify what needs improvement. ✅ 10. Iterate & Scale Use real feedback to build the next version instead of making assumptions. ➡️ The goal of an MVP is not perfection. ➡️ The goal is learning. ➡️ Build fast. ➡️ Launch early. ➡️ Learn quickly. ➡️ Improve continuously. ✅ That’s how successful products are created. You have an idea? Let’s turn your idea into a MVP. 📩 DM us for further details! MVP Startup ProductDevelopment WebDevelopment SaaS

Caption: 𝐏𝐀𝐍𝐄𝐋 𝐃𝐈𝐒𝐂𝐔𝐒𝐒𝐈𝐎𝐍 | 𝐈𝐍𝐃𝐈𝐀 𝐖𝐀𝐑𝐄𝐇𝐎𝐔𝐒𝐈𝐍𝐆 𝐒𝐇𝐎𝐖 𝟐𝟎𝟐𝟔 | 𝐃𝐀𝐘 𝟐 As supply chains become increasingly digital, industry experts came together to discuss "𝐀𝐮𝐭𝐨𝐦𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐀𝐈: 𝐒𝐜𝐚𝐥𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐁𝐞𝐲𝐨𝐧𝐝 𝐏𝐫𝐨𝐨𝐟 𝐨𝐟 𝐕𝐚𝐥𝐮𝐞," exploring how businesses can move from AI experimentation to enterprise-wide transformation. The session highlighted the need to make AI and automation foundational by building a strong data-driven culture that enables smarter decision-making, operational efficiency, and greater resilience. Caption: 🔐 **Why is Anti-Money Laundering (AML) the first line of defense in today's financial landscape?** Financial crimes are becoming increasingly sophisticated, making traditional monitoring no longer enough. According to PPATK 2025 data, suspicious transaction reports have reached **43.72 million** (+25.5% YoY), while analyzed funds have exceeded **Rp 2,085.48 trillion** (+42.88% YoY). Money laundering often follows recognizable patterns, including: • Structuring – splitting large transactions to avoid reporting thresholds. • Layering – moving funds across multiple accounts to obscure their origin. • Integration – reintroducing illicit funds into the legitimate economy. • Shell Companies – using business entities to disguise ownership and transactions. • Cash-Out – quickly withdrawing and transferring funds to erase digital traces. To combat these evolving threats, organizations need an AML solution that goes beyond rule-based detection. Modern AML platforms should provide: ✅ Real-time cross-reference ✅ Network analysis ✅ Real-time risk scoring ✅ Pattern recognition ✅ Automated reporting to regulatory authorities The future of financial security starts with the right infrastructure. AI-powered AML solutions help financial institutions detect suspicious activities faster, reduce false positives, and stay compliant in an increasingly complex regulatory environment. At **ShiFor**, we're committed to helping organizations build smarter fraud detection and AML capabilities with AI-driven technology. Caption: 🔄 What makes AI Agents different from chatbots? The answer is AI Loops. Instead of responding once and stopping, AI agents follow a continuous cycle: 👀 Observe → 🧠 Think → 🔧 Act → ✅ Evaluate → 🔄 Repeat This loop allows agents to improve decisions, use tools, and keep working until a goal is achieved. ✈️ Example Ask an AI agent: “Find me the cheapest flight to Delhi.” The agent will: 1. Search available flights 2. Compare prices and timings 3. Evaluate the best option 4. Search again if a better deal exists 5. Return the optimal result That’s an AI loop in action. 🚀 Why AI Loops Matter ✅ Handle complex multi-step tasks ✅ Learn from feedback and results ✅ Improve decision-making over time ✅ Enable autonomous AI agents ✅ Power systems like Claude Code, Cursor, CrewAI, LangGraph, and OpenAI Agents 💡 Key Insight: Traditional AI gives answers. Agentic AI uses loops to achieve outcomes. The future of AI isn’t just generating text—it’s reasoning, acting, evaluating, and continuously improving. AI AIAgents AgenticAI crewai

Caption: Tax season may never feel the same again 🤖📄 For many Italians, taxes mean stress, missing receipts, and the fear of forgetting a deduction. But behind the scenes, AI is quietly changing the way accountants work. Instead of spending hours on manual data entry, accountants can now use AI tools with advanced OCR and machine learning to read, organize, and interpret documents faster. The real shift? AI is not here to replace professionals. It can help free them from repetitive tasks so they can become more strategic advisors. Here’s what this could mean for taxpayers: ✅ Fewer human oversights by cross-checking invoices, medical expenses, transfers, and other documents ⚡ Faster processing, turning tasks that once took days into hours 💡 More personalized advice, helping identify tax relief opportunities that might otherwise be missed But there’s a big “if.” AI in tax work must be handled with privacy, security, and human judgment at the center. Sensitive financial data needs strong protection. And because AI can sometimes produce confident but incorrect answers, accountants still need to review, interpret, and take responsibility for the final decisions. For citizens and small businesses, the takeaway is practical: 📌 Ask your accountant what digital tools they use and how your data is protected 📌 Keep documents organized in clean digital formats like PDF or XML 📌 Use the time saved on bureaucracy to talk about planning, strategy, and smarter financial decisions AI could help make tax season less painful and more transparent, but only if humans stay in control of ethics, context, and critical thinking. Would you trust AI to help manage your tax documents if a human expert still reviewed everything? Save this post for tax season, share it with a business owner, and comment with your thoughts 👇 ArtificialIntelligence AI TaxTech Accounting DigitalTransformation Innovation FutureOfWork Productivity EverydayAI SmallBusinessTips FinancialPlanning TechForGood MachineLearning Automation DigitalTools BusinessInnovation ItalyBusiness AIInnovation FutureOfFinance SmartBusiness

Caption: 🚨 **AMZN: AWS Raises AI GPU Pricing as Enterprise Demand Surges** Amazon’s AWS is increasing hourly rates for several GPU-powered EC2 Capacity Blocks starting July 1, including P6-B300, P6-B200, P5, P5e, P5en, and P4de instances used for AI training on Nvidia GPUs. The pricing changes reflect growing demand for high-performance AI infrastructure, with upfront reservation fees and separate operating system charges also applying. 📈 Enterprise demand remains strong. A recent CIO survey found that **56% of technology leaders plan to increase AWS spending in 2026**, while **68% already have dedicated AI budgets**. Overall cloud spending is expected to rise by around **10% next year**, with AWS continuing to rank among the leading cloud providers. 💡 Higher GPU pricing highlights the ongoing shortage of premium AI computing resources as companies race to build and deploy generative AI applications. For Amazon, stronger AI demand could support AWS revenue growth, although rising infrastructure costs may pressure customers seeking large-scale AI capacity. Caption: 🎤 A big thank you to Darshan Dinesh Bhandary for leading an engaging hands-on workshop on "Any Cloud, Locally: Hands-on with Floci Cloud Emulators" at HMJun26. Participants gained practical experience using Floci, a local AWS cloud emulator, to build and test cloud-native applications without relying on live cloud infrastructure. The workshop covered working with core AWS services, including S3, SQS, SNS, DynamoDB, and Lambda, while exploring event-driven architectures, serverless development, and local testing workflows. Attendees also learned how to integrate Floci into development and CI pipelines using Docker and Testcontainers, making cloud development faster, more cost-effective, and easier to test locally. Caption: Producing semiconductors requires vast amounts of ultra-clean water. Kumamoto is known for its abundant groundwater resources and some of the highest-quality water in Japan. In Taiwan, water shortages have occasionally affected semiconductor production. By contrast, Kumamoto offers a stable and reliable water supply, making it an ideal location for one of the world's largest semiconductor manufacturers. Behind TSMC's decision to invest in Kumamoto is strong support from the Japanese government. At the same time, institutions such as Kumamoto University and local technical colleges have launched new semiconductor education programs to train the next generation of engineers. Not only companies, but also, government agencies and educational institutions are working together to build a sustainable ecosystem that supports the semiconductor industry. Caption: At the MSME Growth Conclave 2026, our CEO, Kartik Narayan joined industry leaders Nitin Dave, Rakesh Patil, Nagaraju S and Bharat Kumar for a discussion on the future of talent, skilling and labour in India's MSME economy. Reflecting on the realities of hiring at India's scale, Kartik outlined three imperatives for building a stronger workforce ecosystem: Employment must be viewed through the lens of scale. With nearly 90 crore people in the working-age population, connecting the right talent with the right opportunity requires infrastructure built for India's scale. Retention goes beyond compensation. Sustainable workforce retention comes from creating better employee experiences where people feel respected, supported and have clear pathways for growth. AI is an equalizer, not a replacement. Across services, trading and manufacturing, AI has the potential to democratize access to knowledge and capabilities, enabling MSMEs to become more productive while helping workers continuously build new skills. As India's MSMEs continue to power the country's economic growth, building an ecosystem where businesses can hire with confidence and people can build meaningful careers will remain one of the defining opportunities of this decade. Here are a few glimpses from the event and BusinessLine's coverage of the discussion. msme conclave leadership startup bangalore

Caption: **Most SaaS founders chase ads.** **This founder chased accountants.** When **Srijan Singh** joined his family's deep-tech business, he expected to learn about industrial automation. Instead, he found something surprising. The machines were running perfectly. **The accounting wasn't.** As businesses expanded across multiple locations and compliance became more complex, accounting teams were still spending hours closing books, fixing manual entries and adapting themselves to software that wasn't built for how Indian accountants actually work. Legacy software had become outdated. New-age accounting apps looked modern... ...but many still forced accountants to change their workflow instead of supporting it. Srijan saw an opportunity. That insight became **Kitaabh**—an accounting platform designed around the realities of Indian accounting rather than generic global workflows. But here's what makes the story interesting. He didn't launch expensive marketing campaigns. He didn't spend heavily on paid ads. Instead, he carried the product to **CA conferences and accounting expos.** He sat beside chartered accountants. Watched them use the software. Noticed exactly where they struggled. Improved the product. Then repeated the process again. Every conversation became product research. Every demo became customer validation. ### Why this matters: Many founders try to scale before deeply understanding their users. Srijan did the opposite. He spent less time chasing impressions... ...and more time removing friction. Sometimes the fastest way to grow isn't finding more customers. It's understanding the ones already sitting in front of you. **Question:** If you were building a B2B SaaS startup today, would you spend your first ₹10 lakh on digital marketing—or on meeting 1,000 customers face-to-face? Caption: عوامل هوش مصنوعی می‌توانند کدنویسی کنند، داده‌ها را تحلیل کنند و حتی معامله انجام دهند. اما یک کار ساده را هیچ‌گاه بلد نبودند: پرداخت مستقل هزینه‌ها! هر بار که یک عامل هوش مصنوعی بخواهد از یک API پولی استفاده کند یا داده‌ای خاص بخرد، یک انسان باید وارد عمل شود؛ ثبت‌نام کند، کلید API بسازد و روی دکمه تایید کلیک کند. پروژه Project-r0x این وضعیت را تغییر می‌دهد. این یک ابزار متن‌باز است که به عوامل AI اجازه می‌دهد سرویس‌های آنلاین را پیدا کرده، هزینه‌شان را بپردازند و به‌صورت خودکار از آن‌ها استفاده کنند. این پروژه روی شبکه رابین‌هود و با استیبل‌کوین USDG ساخته شده و از پروتکل x402 قدرت می‌گیرد. روش کار ساده است: عامل درخواست سرویس می‌دهد ← سرور قیمت و آدرس پرداخت را می‌فرستد ← عامل تراکنش را امضا می‌کند ← سرور هزینه را روی شبکه دریافت کرده و نتیجه را برمی‌گرداند. بدون کلید API، بدون اکانت و بدون نیاز به تایید انسان. از طریق پلاگین MCP برای Claude Code، این عوامل به ۱۸ ابزار برای معامله، کسب سود و مدیریت نقدینگی دسترسی دارند. هزینه هر عملیات فقط ۰.۰۱ دلار USDG است. این پروژه هنوز در مراحل اولیه است، اما راه را برای آینده‌ای باز می‌کند که در آن ربات‌ها بدون واسطه با هم تعامل مالی دارند. اگر این ایده همه‌گیر شود، شاید روزی کلیدهای API مثل مودم‌های دایال-آپ قدیمی و از‌رده‌خارج به نظر برسند. به نظر شما پرداخت‌های خودکار بین ربات‌ها، امنیت دنیای دیجیتال را به خطر می‌اندازد یا زندگی را راحت‌تر می‌کند؟ AIAgents Web3 Stablecoins RobinhoodChain x402 CryptoPayments OpenSource DeFi AgentPayments USDG MCP ClaudeCode AutonomousAgents Layer2 Micropayments هوش_مصنوعی ارز_دیجیتال تکنولوژی

Caption: AI coding agents are here, but understanding what they actually *do* to your code is a black box. Until now. Meet Mindwalk, a new open-source tool that’s like a flight recorder for your AI coding assistant. Instead of scrolling through endless text logs to figure out why an agent went off the rails, Mindwalk replays the entire session on an interactive 3D map of your codebase. You can see which files it touched, in what order, and what changes it made. It’s a powerful new way to debug and understand the "thought process" of autonomous agents. This feels like the first step towards a proper observability and debugging stack for AI-driven software development. It’s one of those tools that seems obvious in retrospect, solving a problem many developers are just starting to encounter. The project just launched on GitHub and is gaining traction fast. Go give it a star and try it on your own agent sessions. What's the most confusing thing an AI agent has done to your code? ai opensource developer programming tech innovation coding github devtools aiagents codinglife softwaredevelopment codevisualization debugging mindwalk
Author: _justshipped

Caption: AI agents can write code, analyze data, and trade crypto. But they've never been able to do something far simpler: pay for things on their own. Every time an agent wants to call a paid API or query a premium data feed, a human has to step in. Sign up. Generate an API key. Click approve. Project-r0x changes that. It's an open-source toolkit that lets AI agents discover, pay for, and use online services autonomously. Built on Robinhood Chain using USDG stablecoin, it's powered by the x402 protocol — a revival of HTTP 402 "Payment Required." Here's how it works: An agent requests a service → the server responds with a price and payment address → the agent signs a gasless transaction → the server settles it on-chain → returns the result. No API keys. No accounts. No human approval. Through the MCP plugin for Claude Code, agents get 18 tools for trading, yield, bridging, and liquidity management. Every paid skill costs a flat $0.01 USDG. The project is early-stage with real tradeoffs, but it represents something genuinely new: a working implementation of agent-to-service payments without human intermediation. If this vision plays out, API keys might one day look as outdated as dial-up modems. This is exactly the kind of infrastructure improvement that compounds over time — lower costs today mean more budget for innovation tomorrow. Caption: Day 90 of my 111daysoflearningforchange 🚀 Today I learned about Data Protection and Compliance in AWS, focusing on how organizations classify sensitive data, apply appropriate security controls, and comply with global regulations such as PCI DSS, HIPAA, and GDPR. Why it matters: Data is one of the most valuable assets of any organization. Proper classification, governance, encryption, and compliance help protect sensitive information, maintain customer trust, and satisfy legal and regulatory requirements. Key concepts: 🔹 Data Classification Not all data requires the same level of protection. Organizations classify data based on its sensitivity, such as: Public – Safe to share openly Internal – Intended for organizational use only Confidential – Highly sensitive information requiring strict access controls Proper classification helps determine the appropriate security measures, monitoring, and access policies. 🔹 Data Governance Data governance ensures that sensitive information is: Properly protected throughout its lifecycle Accessible only to authorized users Monitored and audited continuously Managed according to organizational policies and legal requirements 🔹 Compliance Standards AWS provides services and controls that help organizations meet industry regulations, including: • PCI DSS – Protects payment card information through encryption, logging, and strong access controls. • HIPAA – Safeguards healthcare and patient information by enforcing confidentiality, encryption, and auditing. • GDPR – Protects personal data of EU citizens through privacy controls, consent management, and data protection requirements. 🔹 AWS Security Services AWS supports data protection using services such as: Amazon S3 Amazon EBS Amazon RDS Amazon DynamoDB AWS Key Management Service (KMS) AWS CloudHSM IAM AWS CloudTrail Amazon CloudWatch These services help secure data through encryption, key management, access control, monitoring, backups, and auditing. Caption: 🚀 Day 11/365 – CDC vs Full Load Both CDC (Change Data Capture) and Full Load are used to move data—but choosing the right one depends on your use case. 🔄 CDC ✅ Transfers only changed records ✅ Faster and more efficient ✅ Ideal for frequently changing data ✅ Near real-time synchronization 📦 Full Load ✅ Transfers the entire dataset every time ✅ Simple to implement ✅ Best for initial loads or small datasets ❌ Higher processing time and resource usage 💡 Rule of thumb: 📌 Initial migration? → Full Load 📌 Ongoing updates? → CDC Understanding this difference helps you build faster and more scalable data pipelines. Tomorrow, we'll explore Data Orchestration and see how multiple pipelines are scheduled and managed. Caption: Don't Just Monitor Data. Monitor the Right Metric. Setting up alerts is easy. Setting up meaningful alerts is what makes a monitoring system effective. In Databricks SQL Alerts, you can monitor aggregated metrics instead of individual records. This allows you to detect trends and anomalies that matter, rather than reacting to every data point. For example, if you're monitoring sensor data, you can calculate the average temperature for each sensor and trigger an alert only when the average exceeds a threshold for multiple consecutive executions. This approach helps you: ✔️ Reduce false alarms caused by temporary spikes ✔️ Focus on sustained issues instead of one-off events ✔️ Improve the reliability of operational monitoring ✔️ Receive notifications only when action is actually needed By combining aggregations, thresholds, and consecutive alert conditions, you can build monitoring systems that are both accurate and practical for production environments. 💡 Key Takeaway Effective monitoring isn't about generating more alerts—it's about generating the right alerts. Designing alerts around aggregated metrics and consecutive evaluations helps surface real issues while reducing unnecessary noise. Caption: Microsoft Fabric e Databricks: mais semelhantes do que parecem. Após meu primeiro mês atuando intensamente com Microsoft Fabric, uma das primeiras percepções que tive foi o quanto sua arquitetura compartilha conceitos com o Databricks. Ao longo da minha trajetória como Engenheiro de Dados, tive a oportunidade de trabalhar com arquiteturas modernas de dados em diferentes ecossistemas de nuvem. Essa experiência tornou a curva de aprendizado do Fabric muito mais natural, pois diversos conceitos já faziam parte do meu dia a dia. Algumas semelhanças que mais chamaram minha atenção: * Arquitetura Lakehouse como base para dados analíticos; * Processamento distribuído com Apache Spark; * Desenvolvimento utilizando Notebooks, Pipelines e SQL; * Governança e linhagem de dados; * Integração com modelos de IA e Machine Learning; * Foco em escalabilidade, performance e engenharia de dados moderna. Na prática, percebi que o conhecimento adquirido anteriormente em plataformas como o Databricks não apenas é reaproveitado, como também acelera significativamente a adoção do Microsoft Fabric. Nesse primeiro mês na Axia Energia, tenho participado da construção e evolução de soluções utilizando Lakehouse, Data Pipelines, Dataflows, Notebooks, Semantic Models, além de iniciativas voltadas para governança, monitoramento e otimização de performance. Cada plataforma possui suas particularidades e fortalezas, mas os fundamentos da engenharia de dados moderna permanecem os mesmos. Quando entendemos bem esses conceitos, a mudança de tecnologia deixa de ser um desafio e passa a ser uma evolução natural. Estou bastante motivado para continuar explorando todo o potencial do Microsoft Fabric e aprofundando ainda mais meus conhecimentos nesse ecossistema. Caption: One of the biggest reasons Snowflake became the preferred cloud data platform is its unique architecture. Instead of tightly coupling storage and compute like traditional data warehouses, Snowflake separates them. This simple architectural decision enables organizations to scale workloads independently, improve performance, reduce costs, and support multiple users simultaneously without resource contention. In this post, we break down this concept using a simple restaurant analogy so that anyone—from beginners to experienced professionals—can understand how Snowflake delivers speed, flexibility, and scalability. Caption: Batch reCAPTCHA form submissions need more than just solving tokens. When you are processing multiple forms, solving one token at a time can slow the entire workflow. A better approach is to use a token pipeline: solve tokens in the background, keep a small fresh buffer, and submit forms as tokens become available. But token freshness matters. reCAPTCHA tokens are short-lived and should be used quickly. If your buffer is too large, tokens may expire before submission. If it is too small, your form pipeline may stall while waiting for new tokens. A reliable pipeline should include: • Fresh token queue • Small buffer size • One token per form submission • Token age checks • Separate pools for different sitekeys • Session and IP consistency • Monitoring for expired or rejected tokens The goal is not to stockpile tokens. The goal is to deliver the right fresh token to the right form at the right time. This type of workflow should only be used in owned, client-authorized, or contractually permitted environments. automationengineering Recaptcha CaptchaAI PythonAutomation DevOps APIMonitoring WorkflowAutomation DataEngineering FormAutomation EngineeringTips

Caption: Your database crashes mid-write. Your data survives. Know why? The Write-Ahead Log (WAL) is the quiet hero behind every reliable database: every change is written to an append-only log on disk BEFORE the actual data files are touched, so a crash can never lose a committed transaction. Swipe through to see: 1. The problem WALs solve and how they solve it 2. The commit path, step by step (write, fsync, ack, apply, checkpoint) 3. Where WALs run in production: PostgreSQL, Kafka, MySQL, RocksDB and more Save this post for your next system design interview, follow for a new system design topic every day, and share it with a developer who needs it. Caption: Data doesn’t move itself. Here’s what a data pipeline actually is, minus the jargon. A pipeline moves data from source to useful, on a schedule, again and again. Every number you trust rode one to get there. It runs in four stages: ingest, store, transform, serve. Ingest pulls data out of every source, APIs, databases, event streams, files, using tools like Fivetran, Airbyte, or Kafka. Transform turns raw into trustworthy: clean, dedupe, join, model, usually with dbt or Spark. (Transform before loading is ETL; after is ELT.) Then something has to run it on time, so Airflow, Dagster, or Prefect schedule the steps as a DAG and shout when one fails. That scheduling is the real job. The 9am fire drill you’ll recognize: “why is the dashboard showing yesterday’s numbers?” Nine times out of ten a pipeline broke upstream, a source changed or a job died quietly at 2am. That’s why monitoring matters, and why good pipelines are boring: they run on time, fail loudly, and never make you doubt the numbers. What’s your pipeline stack, Airflow, Dagster, or something else? Send this to whoever owns the 2am call. [ DataEngineering , Databricks , spark , Airflow , Dbt, DataPipeline ] DataEngineering Airflow dbt ETL DataPipeline

Caption: Data engineers build the pipelines everyone depends on and nobody can see. Data engineering has the same visibility problem as reliability work, sharpened. Your pipelines power the dashboards, models, and decisions across the whole company, yet the work itself is infrastructure, invisible by design when it runs well. Which makes the individual-impact test genuinely hard. "I built the data platform" is true and, on its own, unpersuasive. An assessor needs to see what your platform enabled and what specifically you contributed. The strong version quantifies and attributes. The pipeline or platform you designed and the measurable outcomes it unlocked: analytics that drove decisions, models it made possible, scale it sustained, cost or latency it cut. Tooling you built that other teams or the community adopted. Open-source contributions to the data ecosystem with real uptake. Talks and writing that shaped how others build data systems. The move that works is connecting your infrastructure to the business outcomes it made possible, then attributing the design to you. A £200 Fit Assessment helps a data engineer surface the downstream impact of foundational work and builds an evidence plan around it. Caption: Why AI models need billions of parameters. You hear terms like '70-billion parameter model' constantly. But what actually is a parameter? In neural networks, parameters are the mathematical knobs (weights and biases) that determine how signals propagate. Here is why models require billions of parameters to operate: 🎛️ Static Rules vs. Knobs: Traditional software uses hand-coded if/else rules, which break under language complexity. Neural networks replace rules with parameters: dynamic float values adjusting variables automatically. 🧮 Weights & Biases: Every parameter is either a Weight (w) scaling input signal strengths, or a Bias (b) shifting output thresholds, forming the linear equation y = wx + b at each node. 📈 Scaling the Complexity Wall: Mapping a line requires 2 parameters. Mapping the English language (grammar, coding logic, facts, tone, sarcasm) requires billions of parameter slots to hold conceptual connections. 📚 Deep Layer Abstractions: Parameters are stacked. Shallow layers map basic structures like letters. Middle layers track grammar. Deep layers map logical reasoning and complex programming syntax. 🔧 Tuning Billions of Knobs: During training, backpropagation compares guesses to target text, updating weights by tiny fractions using gradients to minimize errors. Save this post to simplify machine learning fundamentals! 💾 Follow to master AI engineering, one bite-sized post at a time. 🚀 Next up: We'll share a visual sheet mapping forward and backward propagation calculations. aiengineering parameters deeplearning machinelearning neuralnetworks mathematics backpropagation dataengineering coding python realaiengineering

Caption: Micro vs. macro influencers: which one actually pays off? 👇 Everyone chases follower count. But engagement — not audience size — is what drives ROI. 🔹 Micro-influencers (10K–100K): ~6% engagement, $250–$1.5K per post, ~$0.05 per engaged follower. Small, trusting, niche audiences. 🔹 Macro-influencers (1M+): ~1.5% engagement, $10K–$100K+ per post, $1–$2 per engaged follower. Big reach, lower intimacy. Same $25K budget → 50 micro-influencers can out-deliver 1 macro-influencer on engaged reach. The move: use macro for awareness, micro for trust and conversions. Most brands need both — just not in equal measure. Which one is your brand leaning on right now? Tell us below 👇 Follow for more no-fluff marketing breakdowns. Caption: Most deals don't die because of price. They die because trust disappears. Recently, I came across a simple framework in Ryan Serhant's Sell It Like Serhant that reinforced something I've experienced throughout my career in B2B sales, SaaS, AI, and enterprise technology. The principles are timeless because they reflect how people make buying decisions. Here are six reasons deals quietly fall apart: You fail to communicate clearly. You react instead of responding with intention. You create expectations your team can't deliver. You don't understand your customer's business deeply enough. Your approach has become stale and predictable. You're more focused on your commission than on your customer's outcome. Notice something? None of these have anything to do with price. Or discounts. Or "closing techniques." Sales is rarely won by the person who talks the most. It's won by the person who creates the most certainty. Reading Sell It Like Serhant reminded me that while technology keeps evolving, the fundamentals of great selling don't. That's one of the ideas behind BoringSales. AI can help you scale. Automation can help you move faster. But neither replaces trust, curiosity, preparation, or genuine expertise. The best sales professionals don't convince people to buy. They remove the reasons not to buy. I'd love to hear your perspective. Which of these six deal killers do you encounter most often? 1️⃣ Poor communication 2️⃣ Unrealistic expectations 3️⃣ Lack of product or industry knowledge 4️⃣ Generic sales approach 5️⃣ Being too focused on the money 6️⃣ Something else? Share your answer in the comments. Let's compare notes.

Caption: What if ChatGPT is reinforcing the very relationship patterns you're trying to heal? The biggest limitation of AI isn't intelligence. It's unconsciousness. It can only work with the parts of you that make it into the prompt. It cannot see the unconscious survival patterns shaping those words in the first place. Therefore, inadvertently, AI can become an extraordinarily sophisticated mirror of the very patterns we don't yet recognize in ourselves. Emotional freedom doesn't begin with better prompts or answers. It begins when the unconscious patterns shaping your perception become visible. But it doesn't end there. Real transformation happens when you meet the core fears those patterns have been protecting all along. That's where your relationships begin to change. I unpack this much more deeply in my latest article, still trending on The Good Men Project. 🔗 Link in bio. Caption: Apple is suing OpenAI for allegedly stealing trade secrets. Not a copyright dispute. Not a patent claim. Trade secrets, directed by senior OpenAI leadership, involving a former Apple employee who crossed over. This is the kind of story that sounds dramatic until you realize what it actually signals. The competition for AI talent is so intense that companies are willing to torch relationships and go to court over who knows what. Apple has been quietly building its own AI infrastructure for years. Apple Intelligence, on-device models, the deal with OpenAI for Siri. And now they're suing the same company they partnered with. That tension makes sense if you think about it. Partnerships in AI right now are temporary. Everyone is racing to own the full stack. Apple does not want to depend on OpenAI forever. OpenAI does not want to be a backend for Apple products forever. The lawsuit is just the public version of a war that was already happening. For builders, the takeaway is not about the legal drama. It's about what this says about the underlying models and infrastructure you're building on. The companies behind your tools are fighting over talent, IP, and positioning. That instability is real. Diversify what you build on. Never go all-in on one provider's ecosystem if you can avoid it. The AI layer wars are just getting started. The big players are circling each other, and the collateral damage lands on developers who built deep dependencies into one company's stack. What's your current setup? Are you building on one provider or spreading across multiple? claudecode aiagents buildwithAI llm aitools artificialintelligence

Caption: MCP n'est pas qu'un acronyme de plus. C'est la fin des silos IA en entreprise. Google DeepMind vient d'intégrer le support MCP (Model Context Protocol) directement dans l'API Gemini pour ses agents managés. En clair : vos bases de données internes et vos APIs peuvent maintenant nourrir Gemini exactement comme elles nourrissent déjà Claude. Ce n'est pas un détail technique. C'est un changement de paradigme. Jusqu'ici, changer de modèle IA signifiait souvent repartir de zéro sur l'intégration des données. Connecteur propriétaire, format différent, logique d'accès à réécrire. Une dette d'infrastructure invisible mais réelle. Avec MCP comme standard commun, la logique s'inverse : → Vous construisez une infrastructure de données une seule fois → Elle devient compatible avec Claude, Gemini, et les modèles qui suivront → Votre choix de modèle devient une décision business, pas une contrainte technique C'est exactement ce qu'on observe sur le terrain : les équipes qui ont investi tôt dans une couche MCP propre changent de modèle sans friction. Les autres refont le travail à chaque migration. L'interopérabilité n'est pas sexy à vendre. Mais elle définit la durée de vie réelle de votre architecture IA. Construire sur un standard ouvert, c'est choisir de ne pas se faire enfermer. Caption: # Humanoid Robots in Daily Life: How Human-Like Machines Are Transforming the Future Humanoid robots are no longer confined to science fiction. Thanks to rapid advancements in artificial intelligence (AI), robotics, and machine learning, these human-like machines are beginning to play meaningful roles in everyday life. From assisting customers in stores to supporting healthcare professionals, humanoid robots are changing how we live and work. ## What Are Humanoid Robots? Humanoid robots are designed to resemble the human body, often featuring a head, torso, arms, and legs. They use AI, sensors, cameras, and advanced software to understand their surroundings, interact with people, and perform a wide range of tasks. Unlike traditional industrial robots, humanoid robots are built to operate in environments designed for humans, making them ideal for homes, hospitals, offices, and public spaces. ## Everyday Uses of Humanoid Robots ### 1. Healthcare Assistance Humanoid robots can assist nurses and caregivers by delivering medicine, transporting supplies, monitoring patients, and providing companionship for elderly individuals. They help reduce workloads while improving patient care. ### 2. Customer Service Many hotels, airports, banks, and retail stores are using humanoid robots to greet visitors, answer common questions, provide directions, and improve customer experiences. ### 3. Home Support Future home robots may help with household chores such as cleaning, organizing, reminding family members about appointments, and even assisting with meal preparation. ### 4. Caption: The simplest architecture consists of a single agent. One agent receives the user's request, determines what actions are required, calls the necessary tools, retrieves information when appropriate, and generates a response. For many business applications, this architecture is entirely sufficient. Customer support. Knowledge assistants. Internal search. Document summarization. Simple workflow automation. These applications often benefit from a single well-designed agent rather than a collection of specialized agents. Simplicity is frequently the most effective optimization strategy. As AI applications become more sophisticated, organizations often divide responsibilities among multiple specialized agents. One agent performs research. Another retrieves documents. Another analyzes financial information. Another writes reports. Another validates outputs. Another reviews compliance requirements. Specialization frequently improves maintainability and allows different models or prompts to be optimized for different tasks. However, specialization also introduces additional infrastructure. Every agent requires reasoning. Every interaction between agents generates additional inference. Messages must be exchanged. Context must be transferred. Memory must be synchronized. The architecture becomes increasingly distributed. Cloud costs increase accordingly. The question therefore should never be how many agents can be deployed. It should be how many agents are genuinely required. seawingai.com Read more in this book Reducing AI Cloud Spend - ai ml aws azure gcp cloud cfo ceo coo

Caption: Most AI projects never make it to production. Not because the models aren't smart, but because the infrastructure isn't ready. We’re currently in the "Proof of Concept" graveyard. Companies are spending millions on LLMs, agents, and RAG pipelines, but they’re often building them on foundations designed for 2020 web apps. The result? → Unpredictable latency. → Sky-high inference costs. → Security gaps that remain invisible until it’s too late. Caption: 2026년 220번째 서적 🙋‍♂”여전히 부동의 1위 AWS”’ _ 클라우드 시장(Iaas)의 부동의 1위는 AWS(아마존 웹 서비스)입니다. 올해 6월기준 전세계 시장 점유율 38%정도를 유지하고 있고 그 다음이 마이크로소프트의 Azure입니다. 세번째가 구글 클라우드가 될 것이니 클라우드 서비스를 이해한다는 것은 적어도 구글 클라우드 이상부터 학습을 해야한다는 것을 의미합니다. 실제 데이터 시각화의 경우 세일즈포스의 태블로와 MS의 Power BI가 양분하는 것처럼 클라우드 서비스와 유관 인프라 학습은 아마존의 AWS를 학습해야 하며, 관련 인프라에 대한 관심으로 를 읽어보게 되었습니다. AWS는 클라우드 컴퓨팅 서비스입니다. 해당 서비스는 실제 기능은 유사할 수 있으나 UI나 UX 그리고 접근방식과 디테일에서 각사별 차이가 존재합니다. 본서는 클라우드 컴퓨팅의 개념부터 계정가입(Free Tier)부터 보안설정, 그리고 가성서버의 운영 플랫폼인 EC2와 IAM와 네트워킹 서비스를 비롯하여 권한 및 보안관리까지 서적의 제목그래도 AWS의 기본적인 구조부터 활용방법까지를 다루고 있습니다. 비관련자분들에게는 본서의 내용이 상당히 딱딱할 수 있어서 친절한 서적은 아니라고 생각합니다. 그래서 본서는 입에 쓴 좋은 약에 가깝습니다. 다만 클라우드 인프라와 유관업계에서 AWS관련 자격증 보유나 AWS를 통해 본인의 포트폴리오에 유의미한 경력을 넣는 것은 조금 다른 차원의 이슈입니다. 예를 들어 대형 클라우드 사업자가 아니더라도, 개인이 app을 만들고 운영할때 많은 트래픽과 스토리지, 데이터 사용량이 있어서 AWS를 사용하고 관련 확장을 해보았다면 이는 개인적으로도 도움이되고, 실무경험으로도 도움이 되는 부분입니다. 제가 본서를 읽은 것은 현업과 관련된 내역과 개인적은 활용때문이고 본서는 최신의 AWS내용과 콘솔까지를 잘 반영한 부분이라 앞으로도 발췌독하면서 유용한 부분을 이용할 계획입니다. ’글로벌 표준‘ 📖AWS교과서 ✒김원일 📍길벗 AWS 아키텍쳐

Caption: Think df.dropDuplicates() is saving your PySpark pipeline? Think again. ❌ When an interviewer asks you to handle duplicate records based on specific criteria—like keeping the most recent order or the highest transaction amount—using dropDuplicates() on a subset of columns is a massive red flag. 🚩 Because Apache Spark is a distributed framework, rows are shuffled and processed across multiple cluster nodes. Without explicit sorting, dropDuplicates() picks a row non-deterministically. You have zero control over which record stays and which one gets thrown away. It might work on a tiny mockup dataset, but it will corrupt your data at scale. To pass the senior data engineer test, you must use a Window specification (Window.partitionBy().orderBy()) paired with row_number(). This approach lets you cleanly isolate the exact record you need, making your code bulletproof and deterministic. Caption: Exploring Kafka's Internal Architecture: Delivering Scalable, Fault-Tolerant Messaging Apache Kafka has become the backbone of modern real-time data platforms, enabling organizations to process millions of events reliably and efficiently. Here are the key building blocks behind Kafka's architecture: Topics & Partitions: Organize and distribute data for parallel processing and horizontal scalability. Producers & Consumers: Producers publish events, while consumers process them independently, enabling decoupled system design. Brokers & Clusters: Multiple brokers work together to provide high availability, scalability, and fault tolerance. Replication: Data is replicated across brokers, ensuring durability and minimizing data loss during failures. Event Streaming: Power real-time analytics, IoT, fraud detection, logging, and microservices with continuous event processing. Kafka Connect & Kafka Streams: Simplify data integration and enable real-time stream processing with minimal effort. Kafka's distributed architecture, durability, and high throughput make it a preferred platform for building resilient, event-driven applications at scale. Where have you used Kafka, log aggregation, event streaming, microservices, or real-time analytics? Share your experience! Caption: Your brand isn't broken. Your marketing isn't broken. Your customer journey is broken. Here's why that matters. I've worked with 40+ businesses this year. Different industries. Different sizes. Same root problem — every time. The journey from "stranger" to "paying customer" was broken. Not dramatically. Quietly. A confusing step here. A missing trust signal there. A message that speaks to the wrong person at the wrong moment. And every broken step costs money. Silently. Consistently. Here's what a broken customer journey looks like: → Ads perform well. Conversions don't follow. → Demos get booked. Deals don't close. → Trials activate. Users don't return. → Customers buy once. They don't come back. The brand is fine. The marketing is fine. But somewhere between "first impression" and "payment" — trust breaks. What fixes it? Not a rebrand. Not more ad spend. Not a new feature. A CX audit. AI-powered mapping of every touchpoint in your customer journey. Finding the exact moment trust breaks — and fixing it. That's what I do. For SaaS. For e-commerce. For digital products. For service businesses. The business that maps its journey first — wins. Where does trust break in your customer journey? I can show you. DM me 'CX' to start. Caption: "Mon site est sur Google." D'accord. Mais sur quelle page ? Parce qu'être sur Google et être VU sur Google, ce sont deux mondes différents. La page 2 de Google, c'est le meilleur endroit pour cacher un corps. Personne n'y va jamais. Voici un auto-diagnostic gratuit. 5 symptômes. Coche mentalement les tiens. → Tu apparais sur Google, mais jamais en première page. → Tu as du trafic, mais il ne se transforme pas en demandes. → Tes pages n'ont pas de titres ni de descriptions travaillés. → Tes concurrents te passent devant sur les mêmes recherches. → Tu sens que ton site pourrait ressortir bien plus haut. Un ou deux symptômes ? Rien de grave, tu ajustes. Trois ou plus ? Tu laisses des places (et des clients) en haut de Google. Le plus important : tu peux commencer à corriger ce soir, seul. Ta première action, la plus rentable : 1. Ouvre ton site. 2. Regarde l'onglet de ton navigateur (l'onglet en haut). Le texte qui s'affiche, c'est ton "titre" (title). Le résumé sous ton lien dans Google, c'est ta "description". 3. Pose-toi une seule question : est-ce qu'ils donnent envie de cliquer ET est-ce qu'ils contiennent le mot que ton client tape ? Exemple concret. Mauvais : "Accueil" Bon : "Plombier à Cotonou - Dépannage rapide 7j/7" Le premier ne dit rien à Google ni au client. Le second dit exactement qui tu es, ce que tu fais, où. Google adore. Le client clique. Fais ça sur tes 5 pages principales cette semaine. C'est gratuit. Ça bouge déjà les lignes. Un site bien réglé, ce n'est pas une dépense. C'est un commercial qui travaille pour toi 24h/24, sans salaire et sans pause. Combien de ces 5 symptômes tu coches, honnêtement ? (Si tu bloques sur le diagnostic, ma messagerie est ouverte. Mais commence par le test au-dessus, il te dira déjà beaucoup.) siteweb seo referencementnaturel marketingdigital benin cotonou ◉ Je te fais gagner plus de clients et plus de chiffre grâce à un site web qui convertit, un référencement précis (SEO & SEA) et une vraie stratégie de croissance. 🐺 Enock ATEDEKON – Développeur SEO & Stratège en Acquisition Clients By DIGITAL SANS DOULEUR™
